claims.claim: Labor costs decreased 0.2% as a percentage of total revenue for the three months ended June 30, 2024 compared to the three months ended June 30, 2023, including 1.5% from sales leverage, partially offset by 1.1% due to restaurant wage inflation, of which 0.5% was due to the minimum wage for restaurants like Chipotle in California increasing to $20 per hour in April 2024.

Labor costs decreased 0.3% as a percentage of total revenue for the six months ended June 30, 2024 compared to the six months ended June 30, 2023, including 1.3% from sales leverage, partially offset by 0.8% due to restaurant wage inflation, of which 0.3% was due to the minimum wage for restaurants like Chipotle in California increasing to $20 per hour in April 2024.

Labor costs decreased 0.4% as a percentage of total revenue for the three months ended March 31, 2025 compared to the three months ended March 31, 2024, including 1.2% from sales leverage, partially offset by 0.8% due to restaurant wage inflation, of which 0.4% was due to the minimum wage for restaurants like Chipotle in California increasing to $20 per hour in April 2024.

Labor costs decreased 0.3% as a percentage of total revenue for the six months ended June 30, 2025 compared to the six months ended June 30, 2024, including 1.2% from sales leverage, partially offset by 0.9% due to restaurant wage inflation, of which 0.4% was due to the minimum wage for restaurants like Chipotle in California increasing to $20 per hour in April 2024.
Marketing expenses increased by 22.1% from $144.5 million in fiscal 2023 to $169.9 million in fiscal 2024, primarily driven by the acquisition of Ruth's Chris.
Marketing expenses increased by 17.6% from $169.9 million in fiscal 2024 to $196.6 million in fiscal 2025, driven by the inclusion of Chuy's.
The marketing expenses for the six months ended November 24, 2024, were $1,136.9 million, which includes the addition of Chuy's.

Labor costs decreased 0.2% as a percentage of total revenue for the three months ended June 30, 2024, including 1.1% due to restaurant wage inflation, of which 0.5% was due to the minimum wage for restaurants like Chipotle in California increasing to $20 per hour in April 2024.

Labor costs increased 0.6% as a percentage of total revenue for the three months ended June 30, 2025, due to the impact from lower sales volumes. A 0.5% benefit from menu price increases in the prior year and efficient management of labor more than offset wage inflation.

Labor costs increased 1.1% as a percentage of total revenue for the three months ended March 31, 2026, primarily driven by a 0.4% impact from costs related to certain legal proceedings, 0.3% from wage inflation, 0.3% from lower average restaurant sales volumes, and 0.2% from higher benefits expense, including performance-based bonuses. These increases were partially offset by a 0.2% benefit from menu price increases.

Labor costs increased 0.6% as a percentage of total revenue for the three months ended March 31, 2025, due to the impact from lower sales volumes, as a 0.7% benefit from menu price increases in the prior year was offset by restaurant wage inflation, including minimum wage increases for our restaurants in California.
Revenue increased to $11.3 billion, a 0.6% change from the previous period.
The provided filings do not discuss revenue for the previous period.
Revenue decreased to $2.9 billion, a 6.5% change from the previous period.
The decrease is attributed to a 11.4% decrease in delivery service revenue, partially offset by a 6.5% increase in food and beverage revenue.
The decrease in delivery service revenue is driven by a 11.4% decrease in revenue, while food and beverage revenue increased by 6.5%.
The increase in food and beverage revenue is driven by a 3.4% increase in average restaurant sales.
Revenue increased to $2,875.3 million, a 6.4% change from the previous period.
The increase is driven by a 6.5% increase in food and beverage revenue and a 11.4% decrease in delivery service revenue.
The increase in food and beverage revenue is driven by a 3.4% increase in average restaurant sales.
Revenue decreased to $3.1 billion, a 7.5% change from the previous period.
The decrease is attributed to a 4.0% decrease in comparable restaurant sales, which is partially offset by a 0.9% increase in average check.
The decrease in comparable restaurant sales is driven by a 4.9% decrease in transactions, while the increase in average check is driven by a 0.9% increase.
Revenue increased to $3.0 billion, a 7.5% change from the previous period.
The increase is attributed to a 0.3% increase in comparable restaurant sales, which is partially offset by a 0.8% decrease in transactions.
The increase in comparable restaurant sales is driven by a 1.1% increase in average check, while the decrease in transactions is driven by a 0.8% decrease.

During the second quarter and first six months of 2025, Cracker Barrel recorded impairment charges of $2,163 and $2,863, respectively, as a result of the deterioration in operating performance in three MSBC locations and two Cracker Barrel locations.
One Cracker Barrel store was closed in the second quarter and first six months of 2025 resulting in closing costs of $288.
No stores were closed in the second quarter or first six months of 2024.

The decrease in professional fees as a percentage of total revenue in the third quarter of 2025 as compared to the same period in the prior year resulted primarily from lower costs related to the Company's strategic transformation plan.

The decrease in incentive compensation expense as a percentage of total revenue in the third quarter of 2025 as compared to the same period in the prior year resulted primarily from Chief Executive Officer transition costs incurred in 2024.
The increase in other store operating expenses as a percentage of total revenue in 2025 as compared to 2024 resulted primarily from costs associated with the Company's off-premise business.
General and administrative expenses increased from 5.9% to 7.1% of total revenue, and impairment and store closing costs remained at 0.1% of total revenue.
The provided filings do not discuss food and beverage costs as a percentage of revenue for the period. (repeated across multiple sources for eval-006)
Comparable restaurant sales decreased 4.0% for the three months ended June 30, 2025. The decrease is attributable to lower transactions of 4.9% which is offset by a 0.9% increase in average check.
Comparable restaurant sales decreased 0.4% for the three months ended March 31, 2025. The decrease is attributable to lower transactions of 2.3% which is offset by a 1.9% increase in average check.

Comparable restaurant sales increased 0.3% for the three months ended September 30, 2025. The increase is attributable to a 1.1% increase in average check, partially offset by lower transactions of 0.8%.
Comparable restaurant sales decreased 1.7% for the year ended December 31, 2025. The decrease is attributable to lower transactions of 2.9%, partially offset by a 1.2% increase in average check.
During the three months ended June 30, 2024, Chipotle opened 52 company-operated restaurants, including 46 with a Chipotlane.
The company is on track to open approximately 285-315 new restaurants in 2024.
It expects that at least 80% of its new restaurants will include a Chipotlane.
During the three months ended September 30, 2024, Chipotle opened 86 company-operated restaurants, including 73 with a Chipotlane.
The company is on track to open approximately 285 to 315 new company-operated restaurants in 2024.
It expects that at least 80% of its new company-operated restaurants will include a Chipotlane.
During the three months ended March 31, 2025, Chipotle opened 57 restaurants, including 48 with a Chipotlane.
The company expects to open approximately 315 to 345 company-owned restaurants in 2025.
It expects that at least 80% of its new company-owned restaurants will include a Chipotlane.
During the three months ended September 30, 2025, Chipotle opened 84 restaurants, including 64 with a Chipotlane.
The company remains on track to open approximately 315 to 345 company-owned restaurants in 2025.
It expects to open approximately 350 to 370 restaurants in 2026, including 10 to 15 international partner-operated restaurants.
It expects that at least 80% of its new company-owned restaurants will include a Chipotlane.
General and administrative expenses decreased by 20.6% from $159.5 million in 2023 to $126.6 million in 2024.

The decrease was driven by stock-based compensation (-$32.0 million), performance bonuses (-$5.7 million), legal contingencies (-$2.2 million), restructuring costs (-$1.4 million), conferences (-$0.3 million), outside services ($1.4 million), wages ($6.7 million), other ($0.6 million).
General and administrative expenses increased by 15.9% from $126.6 million in 2024 to $146.7 million in 2025.

The increase was driven by stock-based compensation ($11.1 million), performance bonuses (-$2.3 million), conferences ($0.3 million), legal contingencies ($2.2 million), legal services ($0.2 million), outside services ($0.1 million), wages ($4.6 million), stock-based comp retention awards ($2.9 million), other ($1.0 million).
General and administrative expenses increased by 17.9% from $172.8 million in 2025 to $203.7 million in 2026.

The increase was driven by conferences ($23.3 million), performance bonuses ($5.9 million), wages ($5.1 million), restructuring ($2.9 million), stock-based comp retention awards (-$8.9 million), other ($2.6 million).
Olive Garden's sales increase for fiscal 2024 was primarily driven by a U.S. same-restaurant sales increase combined with revenue from new restaurants.
LongHorn Steakhouse's sales increase for fiscal 2024 was primarily driven by a same-restaurant sales increase combined with revenue from new restaurants.
Fine Dining's sales increase for fiscal 2024 was driven by the acquisition of Ruth's Chris, offset by same-restaurant sales decreases.
Other Business's sales increase for fiscal 2024 was driven by revenue from new restaurants offset by same-restaurant sales decreases.
Olive Garden's sales increase for fiscal 2025 was primarily driven by a U.S. same-restaurant sales increase combined with revenue from new restaurants.
LongHorn Steakhouse's sales increase for fiscal 2025 was primarily driven by a same-restaurant sales increase combined with revenue from new restaurants.
Fine Dining's sales increase for fiscal 2025 was driven by revenue from new restaurants offset by same-restaurant sales decreases.
Other Business's sales increase for fiscal 2025 was driven by a U.S. same-restaurant sales increase combined with revenue from new restaurants including the acquisition of Chuy's.
Olive Garden's sales increase for the third quarter of fiscal 2025 was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases, as well as revenue from new restaurants.
LongHorn Steakhouse's sales increase for the third quarter and nine months of fiscal 2025 was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases combined with revenue from new restaurants.
Olive Garden's sales increase for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases, as well as revenue from new restaurants.
LongHorn Steakhouse's sales increase for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases, as well as revenue from new restaurants.

Olive Garden's sales increase for the six months of fiscal 2026 was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases, as well as revenue from new restaurants, partially offset by the sale of all eight of our Olive Garden Canada locations.

Food and beverage costs as a percentage of sales decreased in fiscal 2025 by 0.9%, primarily due to a 0.9% impact from pricing leverage and a 0.2% impact from cost savings, partially offset by a 0.5% impact from mix and other.

Food and beverage costs as a percentage of sales decreased in the second quarter and the first six months of fiscal 2025 by 0.9%, primarily due to a 0.9% impact from pricing leverage and a 0.2% impact from cost savings, partially offset by a 0.5% impact from mix and other.
For fiscal 2024, Olive Garden's segment profit margin increased by 90 basis points (BP) to 21.9% compared to 21.0% in fiscal 2023.
The increase was driven primarily by positive same-restaurant sales and lower food and beverage costs and restaurant expenses, partially offset by higher marketing costs.
For the second quarter of fiscal 2025, Olive Garden's segment profit margin increased by 40 BP to 21.4% from 21.0% in the same period of fiscal 2024.
The increase was driven primarily by positive same-restaurant sales, lower food and beverage costs, and restaurant labor costs, partially offset by increased marketing costs.
For the third quarter of fiscal 2026, Olive Garden's segment profit margin decreased by 10 BP to 20.6% from 20.7% in the same period of fiscal 2025.
The decrease was driven primarily by higher restaurant expenses and marketing costs, partially offset by lower restaurant labor costs.
For the first nine months of fiscal 2026, Olive Garden's segment profit margin remained flat at 21.8% compared to 21.8% in the same period of fiscal 2025.

Olive Garden's sales increase for the second quarter of fiscal 2025 was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases and revenue from new restaurants. The increase in U.S. same-restaurant sales resulted from a 3.7 percent increase in average check, partially offset by a 1.6 percent decrease in same-restaurant guest counts.

LongHorn Steakhouse's sales increase was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases and revenue from new restaurants. The increase in same-restaurant sales resulted from a 4.3 percent increase in same-restaurant guest counts and a 3.1 percent increase in average check.

Fine Dining's sales decrease was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales decreases and a decrease in same-restaurant guest counts. The decrease in same-restaurant sales resulted from an 8.3 percent decrease in same-restaurant guest counts, offset by a 2.7 percent increase in average check.

Olive Garden's sales increase [six months FY2025] was primarily driven by revenue from new restaurants, offset by same-restaurant sales decreases. The decrease in U.S. same-restaurant sales resulted from a 3.6 percent decrease in same-restaurant guest counts, partially offset by a 3.3 percent increase in average check.

LongHorn Steakhouse's sales increase [six months] was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases and revenue from new restaurants. The increase in same-restaurant sales resulted from a 3.0 percent increase in average check and a 2.5 percent increase in same-restaurant guest counts.

Fine Dining's sales decrease [six months] was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales decreases and a decrease in same-restaurant guest counts. The decrease resulted from an 8.7 percent decrease in same-restaurant guest counts, partially offset by a 3.1 percent increase in average check.

Olive Garden's sales increase for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 was primarily driven by same-restaurant sales increases and revenue from new restaurants. The increase in U.S. same-restaurant sales resulted from a 1.7 percent increase in same-restaurant guest counts and a 3.0 percent increase in average check, which includes a 1.1 percent increase in off-premise catering sales.

The acquisition of Chuy's was expected to impact Darden's financial results by increasing capital expenditures for building new restaurants, remodeling, and maintaining existing restaurants and for technology initiatives to be approximately $650 million.

The acquisition of Chuy's also impacted Darden's financial results by increasing depreciation and amortization expenses as a percent of sales, primarily due to the acquisition of Chuy's as well as incremental depreciation on new restaurants and other capital investments.
There was an increase in net interest expense as a percent of sales for the third quarter and the first nine months of fiscal 2025, primarily due to financing related to the Chuy's acquisition.
The first quarter of 2025 saw a 2.6% increase in total revenue compared to the same period in the prior year.
This increase is driven by a 2.9% increase in restaurant revenue and a 1.6% decrease in retail revenue.
The average check increase was 5.8%, while comparable restaurant guest traffic decreased by 2.9%.
For the year 2024, total revenue increased by 0.8% compared to 2023.
The restaurant revenue increased by 0.5%, while retail revenue decreased by 5.5%.
The average check increase was 4.9%, and comparable restaurant guest traffic decreased by 5.0%.
In the second quarter and first six months of 2025, total revenue increased by 1.5% and 2.0%, respectively, compared to the same periods in the prior year.
Restaurant revenue increased by 1.5%, and retail revenue increased by 2.0%.
The average check increase was 7.4%, and comparable restaurant guest traffic decreased by 2.7%.
For the second quarter and first six months of 2026, total revenue decreased by 7.9% and 6.8%, respectively, compared to the same periods in the prior year.
Restaurant revenue decreased by 7.1%, and retail revenue decreased by 9.2%.
The average check increase was 3.4%, and comparable restaurant guest traffic decreased by 10.1%.
Cost of goods sold (exclusive of depreciation and rent) increased from 31.7% in 2024 to 32.4% in 2025.
The decrease in total revenue in the second quarter and first six months of 2026, partially offset by lower cost of goods sold expenses, contributed to the operating loss.
The decrease in total revenue in the first nine months of 2026, partially offset by lower cost of goods sold expenses, contributed to the operating loss.
Cost of goods sold (exclusive of depreciation and rent) increased from 24.7% in 2023 to 25.0% in 2024.

The increase in payroll and related expense as a percentage of total revenue in the first nine months of 2026 as compared to the same period in the prior year resulted primarily from severance costs related to a corporate restructuring.

The increase in store hourly labor and store management compensation as a percentage of total revenue in 2024 as compared to 2023 resulted primarily from higher staffing levels and the investment of additional labor hours to improve the guest experience as well as wage inflation partially offset by higher average check.
We presently expect the rate of wage inflation to be approximately 3% to 4% in 2025.
The increase in employee health care expenses as a percentage of total revenue in 2024 as compared to 2023 resulted primarily from higher claims.

The increase in payroll and related expense as a percentage of total revenue in the second quarter and the first six months of 2026 as compared to the same periods in the prior year resulted primarily from severance costs related to a corporate restructuring.
Other store operating expenses decreased from 25.0% to 24.7% of total revenue.
Other store operating expenses increased from 24.5% to 25.3% of total revenue in the third quarter of 2025.

The increase in other store operating expenses for Q3 2025 was primarily due to higher depreciation expense due to higher capital expenditures and higher conference expenses due to a biennial district manager conference held in the first quarter of 2025, costs associated with the off-premise business, and hurricane-related expenses incurred in the first quarter of 2025.
Other store operating expenses increased from 24.0% to 24.4% of total revenue in the first nine months of 2025.

The increase in other store operating expenses for the first nine months of 2025 was primarily due to higher conference expenses due to a biennial district manager conference held in the first quarter of 2025, costs associated with the off-premise business, and hurricane-related expenses incurred in the first quarter of 2025.
Other store operating expenses increased from 25.3% to 25.0% of total revenue in 2025.
The increase in other store operating expenses as a percentage of total revenue in 2025 was primarily due to costs associated with the off-premise business.

Cracker Barrel Old Country Store, Inc. is a publicly traded company that operates and develops the Cracker Barrel Old Country Store® concept. Each Cracker Barrel store consists of a restaurant with a gift shop.

As of September 12, 2025, the Company operated 657 Cracker Barrel stores located in 43 states. The Company also owns Maple Street Biscuit Company, and operated 68 MSBC locations in ten states as of September 12, 2025.
As of January 30, 2026, the Company operated 656 Cracker Barrel stores in 43 states and 54 MSBC locations in ten states.
As of October 31, 2025, the Company operated 656 Cracker Barrel stores in 43 states and 54 MSBC locations in ten states.
As of May 01, 2026, the Company operated 657 Cracker Barrel stores in 43 states and 52 MSBC locations in ten states.

Cracker Barrel's business model is centered around the operation and development of the Cracker Barrel Old Country Store concept, complemented by a gift shop, and has expanded into the MSBC concept, a breakfast and lunch fast casual experience.
General and administrative expenses increased by 6.5% in the second quarter and first six months of 2025 compared to the same periods in 2024.
The increase was primarily driven by higher professional fees related to legal fees and proxy contest expenses, as well as higher costs associated with the Company's strategic transformation plan.
General and administrative expenses increased by 6.0% in 2024 compared to 2023.
Professional fees increased by 0.6% of total revenue; payroll and related expense increased by 0.2%; incentive compensation expense increased by 0.1%.
The increase in professional fees was attributed to the costs associated with the Company's strategic transformation plan.
The increase in payroll and related expense was due to severance costs related to corporate restructuring and Chief Executive Officer transition costs.
The increase in incentive compensation was due to Chief Executive Officer transition costs.
General and administrative expenses increased by 6.2% in 2025 compared to 2024.
Professional fees increased by 0.1% of total revenue; incentive compensation expense increased by 0.1% of total revenue [2025 vs 2024].
The increase in professional fees was attributed to proxy contest expenses and higher legal fees.
The increase in incentive compensation expense was due to higher bonus payouts due to better performance against financial objectives in 2025 compared to 2024.
General and administrative expenses decreased by 1.1% in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same period in 2025.
Incentive compensation expense decreased by 1.0% of total revenue; professional fees decreased by 0.6%; payroll and related expense increased by 0.4% [Q1 2026].
The decrease in incentive compensation expense was due to lower performance against financial objectives in 2026 compared to the prior year period.
